content: Stream created by Mike Rossiter.

Only the shipdriver link works at the moment:
https://rasbats.github.io/ocpn-manuals-test/ocpn-user-plugins-manual/0.1/index.html

What I am doing is creating a module for each plugin. In this way it will be easy to migrate from DokuWiki. Also a new module can be easily extracted from a new plugin manual.

The way that shipdriver is referenced in the dev-manual does not lend itself to inclusion in the pages of the plugins manual.

We could use version number in the antora.yml file for the plugin.

Don't look at the intro text too much. Needs slimming down.

Is this layout going to lend itself to programming links in plugin builds. I should be able to produce a pdf for each plugin from each module with the java stuff.

Link for source repository:
https://github.com/Rasbats/ocpn-user-plugins-manual

Mike, If I configure watchdog similar to shipdriver and fork opcn-user-plugins-manual then make changes and push to you would that get watchdog manual into the system? Is this now setup for plugin documentation properly?

We need to review if the structure I am using is acceptable - one module per plugin.
@Alec Leamas , @Jon , @Rick Gleason ... Will this work for you?

Rick... could you move modules/Root into a manual folder to mirror shipdriver. Yes, you can try a PR but you will see that references need fixing in my ROOT/pages/plugins.adoc as well. If you go ahead it would be a good first step for testing integration with a developer's work.

We might need to think about the structure of the plugin manual folder - perhaps manual/watchdog/modules---/images

                              ---/pages
One module; you mean everything in the user manual repository, but each plugin as a separate module?

No... everything in the ocpn-user-plugins-manual, with each plugin in a separate module there. A link in the user manual would take you to the ocpn-user-plugins-manual, as it does with my ocpn-manuals-test.

I don't really follow you here: "verything in the ocpn-user-plugins-manual, with each plugin in a separate module there. "

Does this mean that ROOT is the main manual, and the other plugins are siblings to ROOT? Or? (I'm probably dumb...)

https://docs.antora.org/antora/2.3/page/version-and-component-xrefs/#xref-page-in-different-version

Example 2 but just the top repo.
Example 5 shows how the link to the module is made.
I'm getting more and more confused. First: what is the meaning of "module" here? And, why are you referring to examples how to link? Is this a problem?

:ledger: repository
:document: antora.yml
:folder: modules
:folder: get-started
:folder: pages
:document: tour.adoc
:folder: la-garita
:folder: pages
:document: cochetopa-pass.adoc
:folder: ROOT
:folder: pages
:document: index.adoc
:document: ranges.adoc

modules (plural) is the starting point. Then there are children in module - get-started, la-garita and ROOT.

Once I have constructed the individual plugin modules, shipdriver, watchdog etc they are then made available to developers by forking the manual. They can be placed in the manual directory of their repo. They can edit from there and make a PR, which would update the manual.

A tool could be supplied to the developer to create a pdf from the module which would be placed in the data directory of the plugin repo. It is easy to add a Help button to the plugin dialog which can open the pdf in a browser. The pdf stays in the data folder alongside the other files like plugin icons, so it gets installed with the plugin.

Looking ahead this structure lends itself to internationisation. Antora is only starting work on this (ADR). But I can see plugin modules in the manual with names like 'shipdriver', 'shipdriver$fr_FR' etc.
Still don't get it. Put it this way: where in that structure, exactly, would for example shipdriver live?

The structure is here:
https://github.com/Rasbats/ocpn-user-plugins-manual
shipdriver is the module name. In the module is shipdriver.adoc in the pages folder and the images folder.
OK, now I understand what you mean. hm...

The core question is how this should be maintained: as a single plugin manual, which plugin devs forks, updates and makes PRs in. Or, that the manual is an integrated part of the plugin repo, maintained by the plugin developer, and just imported into tha main manual.
In other words: should plugin maintainer make a PR against the manual? Or, should the manual maintainer when need be make a PR against the plugin?

I think we start with the user plugins manual. I make the initial modules. These become part of the user plugins manual. Request developers to fork the user plugins manual and copy the plugin module to their repo:
manual-shipdriver-Pages
-images
If they edit they can make a PR and it gets pushed to the main plugins manual.
Developer can make the pdf from the module he has in his repo. Simple!!!!

This is your show. That said, I think you under-estimate the psychology. With these glasses, you propose that plugin developers should fork your manual and contribute changes in PRs. It might be hard to sell. Actually, I'm convinced it's a hard sell

OTOH, if you make an initial PR against the plugin repo with som antora stuff it will cause some initial troubles, for sure. But in the long run, I think at least some plugin devs will get the feeliong that this is their manual which they can maintain and even publish separately if they want.
@Mike Rossiter : we are out of sync, antora seems to have messed up things. Right now, I'm waiting for you to reply -- hope you are not waiting for me...

It is not my manual;) It could live under the github.com/OpenCPN/manuals space. Living in my repo for convenience. Where will they all end up? I think by forking they will feel they have a stake in the manual in the same way as we work with managed plugins which involve a PR that Dave responds to.

That's not the point. The point is that it's not the plugin dev's manual.

Think for a second what the situation would have been the if I have forked shipdriver and implemented everyhting in the fork without making PRs? Would you have been motivated to work with my fork? I bet no...
The plugins manual is just a collection of modules, one for each plugin, nothing more nothing less. It is not the whole manual.

Yes. But if you instead make it a collection of components you get the effect that the plugin is responsible for their own manual. This means that you will need to make PR against plugins which makes the manual part of the plugin rather than a module in your repo. And, I think, we have much better chance to get the plugin devs on board.
But the manual would be part of their plugin. Yes I can make all the PR but we need to get the thing started. The PR would be a request to insert the manual they have already written into their repo.
Porridge calls. Back in a bit.
Same for me . BBL

The workflow would be that you forked some plugin and added the manual parts to the fork. In the main manual you would work with the fork until things are in reasonable shape. When we feel ready, we do PR to the plugins. Not all will be accepted, at least not immediately. For those, we just maintain our forks, much like the sad state of some of the testplugin-based plugins maintained by Rick.

Now it my turn to be confused. How do I work with a fork in the main manual. If you are suggesting a link like you used for shipdriver it will not fly. I do not want 40-50 urls sitting at the bottom of the page. Is it not better to just make the manual from what we have and then fork and make the PR? Some devs have died/lost interest etc
No... I see where this is leading and I do not like it. Working with 40-50 forks is too much work.
And the devs have already done their bit by writing the plugin manual.
The problem with your approach is that , practically, you wont have support from the plugin devs. This no problem initially, but will be over time

Linking is basically no difference, just a somewhat longer xref. The current links at bottom is just a demo, to make sure we can link directly into the compon ent. Certainly not how it is supposed to look later

you wont have support from the plugin devs

I think I have to test this. The alpha version plugins manual can be made very quickly and some feedback gained.
@Rick Gleason Could opencpn.org host this?

"you wont have support from the plugin devs"

Well, if that assumption is true you are of course right. Problem is that if you have that assumptions, there all all chances it will be verified. That damned psychology...

I tend to have more success when I try to work with upstreams rather than just fork & forget. See for example how I managed to get the shipdriver workflow accepted into both radar_pi and oesenc_pi. It was not painless, for sure. But once done, I can just forget them (until they want som help).
BTW: You need no hosting, Github Pages is convenient to use. I'll try to make a PR later so you can publish any build.

The github pages are online:
Only the shipdriver link works at the moment:
https://rasbats.github.io/ocpn-manuals-test/ocpn-user-plugins-manual/0.1/index.html

Hm... zulip fails again (?) Yes, that looks fine.

Just to be clear: you are in charge, since you are doing the job. I just have some opinions.

Consider this a question, to understand what we are doing with plugins.

Isn't ocpn-plugins-manual a "component" , a separate repository that provides a page with links out to each of the plugin repositories for another plugin (singular) "manual" component ?

I have to draw diagrams to understand, really it helps a lot:

github.com/rasbats/ocpn-plugins-doc/modules/plugins-guide/pages/index.adoc
I don't find it there. -not the right place

Plugin Manuals Collection Repository (one)
Here is the html what is the source of this?
https://github.com/Rasbats/ocpn-manuals-test/blob/main/docs/ocpn-user-plugins-manual/1.0/index.html
It looks to me like we could just link from this page to each authors own repository/manuals
Set up like I have done with watchdog.

Plugin Manual Repository (many)
Shipdriver https://rasbats.github.io/ocpn-manuals-test/ocpn-user-plugins-manual/0.1/shipdriver/shipdriver.html
Dashboard-Tactics https://dashboard-tactics-pi.readthedocs.io/en/v2.0.0/
Watchdog (no public html yet, only local)
https://github.com/rgleason/watchdog_pi/blob/master/modules/ROOT/pages/watchdog.adoc

  • I can certainly move this down one level to "manual/modules/ROOT/pages/watchdog.adoc"
  • I can view this nicely with the live.asciidoc.js plugin extension for Firefox, (but without images rendered) so the text documentation is very accessible from within the repository.
  • I could put a link to it in the Readme.md file too this location, or to the public html location.
  • It is easier to set up the build.yml paths and I like keeping the source documentation with the plugin it just makes sense.
  • When building the plugin, we want the documentation to be created at the same time and included in the metadata file.

To generate the html or whatever output for the Plugin Manuals Collection Repository
we can get Jon or Alec to make an Actions script to update the ocpn-manuals page by building each and every plugin manual from the (many) sources and dumping the result in some undetermined public website html location.

  • Then that can be used for Online.

What is the alternative approach?
OK, I will follow this etiquette. Is there somewhere I can refer to this etiquette or is it just kept in programmers DNA?

"For those, we just maintain our forks, much like the sad state of some of the testplugin-based plugins maintained by Rick."
I still hope Sean will accept these once we get each of these plugins updated and working. I am going to continue to make PR's to him, however he has no interest in anything other than what his interest is at the time and the focus is usually Linux only.

Found this in the readme for Mike's Plugins Manual
Committed documents in the docs/ dir are available at https://leamas.github.io/ocpn-manuals/

@Rick Gleason I think you are looking at the wrong repo. The correct one, as shown above is:
https://github.com/Rasbats/ocpn-user-plugins-manual
https://github.com/Rasbats/ocpn-manuals-test/ is the repo which is used to build the manual which is hosted at:
https://rasbats.github.io/ocpn-manuals-test/ocpn-user-plugins-manual/0.1/index.html

What I am working with is a load of modules in the form: manual==shipdriver>images
>pages
shipdriver is a folder with two folders, images and pages. Pages holds shipdriver.adoc
One module for each plugin.

"When building the plugin, we want the documentation to be created at the same time and included in the metadata file."

What documentationn? Yes a link to the website plugin manual. But one idea would be to create a pdf and put that in the plugin data folder ... appdata/local etc.

Did you get the bit about translations and internationalising?

I need to spend a couple of days getting things in place and then you should be able to see what is happening. Bye for now.

You have not explained your concept for how this will work.
Are you planning on forking the watchdog_pi/module/ only into ocpn-user-plugins somehow?

Mike wrote:
What I am working with is a load of modules in the form: manual==shipdriver>images

pages
shipdriver is a folder with two folders, images and pages. Pages holds shipdriver.adoc
One module for each plugin.

Ok how do you get https://github.com/rgleason/watchdog_pi/tree/master/modules/ROOT into ocpn-user-plugins? Is it a link? or is it a fork?

I think it has to be a link!!!!
Give me space for a couple of days please.

Mike wrote:

What documentation? Yes a link to the website plugin manual. But one idea would be to create a pdf and put that in the plugin data folder ... appdata/local etc.

  • Yes, we agree. When the plugin is committed the html or PDF at the public website would be automatically updated.

Did you get the bit about translations and internationalizing?

  • Yes, good point, but I don't understand how to structure that yet.

I need to spend a couple of days getting things in place and then you should be able to see what is happening. Bye for now.

  • OK, same here

I am going to ask kathy if she can host this on opencpn.org which is a good suggestion. What I don't know is how to setup and oauth authentication to allow sftp access for github to upload the new html each time. And this is going to have to happen for each plugin too! It gets complicated I think. It would be better than forcing all plugins devs to host their own github.io just to store their html, but maybe that is the way we have to do it?
@Rick Gleason : He wants some space. Let him have it :)

I think I've figured out that opencpn.org is not the place to host it, too many authentication issues and using oauth keys. Best to use a github.io and have a gatekeeper librarian like Mike accepting the automatic pushes to update the html.

It would be better than forcing all plugins devs to host their own public github.io just to store their html, but maybe that is the way we have to do it?

Pietri is using readthedocs. for Dashboard-Tactics https://readthedocs.org/ Is that a good long term solution? Would cloudsmith work? (probably not) What about a dead simple AWS website account?

Found this on their web

readthedocs

Free docs hosting for open source
We will host your documentation for free, forever. There are no tricks. We help over 100,000 open source projects share their docs, including a custom domain and theme.

https://readthedocs.org/projects/dashboard-tactics-pi/

Look at the downloads page!
https://readthedocs.org/projects/dashboard-tactics-pi/downloads/

Latest Docs
https://dashboard-tactics-pi.readthedocs.io/en/latest/
Darn, I forget I think it uses sphinx

We need to look into Github Pages further, under Products https://docs.github.com/en/github/getting-started-with-github/githubs-products
GitHub Free for user accounts - does not show Github Pages
GitHub Free for organizations - does not show Github Pages
But this page says the both have Github Pages
"Working with Github Pages https://docs.github.com/en/github/working-with-github-pages" "GitHub Pages is available in public repositories with GitHub Free and GitHub Free for organizations

It looks to me like we could have a Free Organization Github Page or a free User Page or a free Project page.
https://docs.github.com/en/github/working-with-github-pages

Since there are many Sean Plugins, what can I do?

Bottom of that github page shown above
"You can only create one user or organization site for each account on GitHub.

  • Project sites, whether owned by an organization or a user account, are unlimited."
    This is great. It appears that github pages is the best alternative considering the authentication issues.
    "Working with Github Pages" https://docs.github.com/en/github/working-with-github-pages

  • "GitHub Pages is available in public repositories with GitHub Free and GitHub Free for organizations,..."

https://docs.github.com/en/github/working-with-github-pages/about-github-pages

  • Use <username>.github.io/project or <organization>.github.io
  • "Unlimited Project files can be added to either type. Source files for a project site are stored in the same repository as their project."
  • "Project sites are available at http(s)://<username>.github.io/<repository> or http(s)://<organization>.github.io/<repository> "

It might be useful use the Organization Github Pages, but I am not sure about collaboration or security keys etc and how it works so I am going to start with

  • <username>.github.io/project
  • <username>.github.io/watchdog_pi/modules (or manual or docs if required)
  • <username>.github.io/weather_routing_pi/modules (or manual or docs if required)
  • <username>.github.io/climatology_pi/modules (or manual or docs if required)
This isn't going to work, because Sean has the Projects, and mine are just forks.

It appears to me that git sub-modules may be a part of the solution
https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Submodules

Sean and Pavel used a submodule for Climatology's Data. Sean made a separate repository for Climatology-Data which contained about 5mb. Then that submodule was referenced in the climatology_pi repository. This could be done for all the separate plugins I think. Not sure how html data would be put into it from another repository. Would we end up with a separate watchdog-doc repository that receives updated html from watchdog_pi when it is built?

Then Mike could use submodules to include that html in his <username>.github.io that is public to have a website that contains all the Plugin Documentation. There are github limits on transfer and file size etc.

Can a github user direct the asciidoc/antora html to land in another repository? YES! We are already doing that with ocpn-dev-manual and ocpn-manuals. The html generated by "antora build.yml lands in ocpn-manuals.

So each plugin dev makes a separate repository called <pluginname_doc> and configures build.yml to put the html in that repository. Then Mike makes that repository watchdog_doc into a submodule in his ocpn-user-plugins-manual so he has access to the updated html.
BTW: To be frank, again. When you post so much text as you have done here, I simply don't read it. Sorry. It's just too much.

I've notice that. Because you ignore parts of short posts too.

Thanks for this..

You should (of course) push your changes on ocpn-manuals to github to make them visible. Probably not much idea to merge things yet (here, I'm upstream)

Maybe Mike can use it as I just suggested above, (after submodules) with a suggestion to use actions to update his ocpn-user-plugin-manuals for each of the remote plugins.

For Petri's [Canne] Dashboard_Tactics_pi
Because it is in sphinx you will need a script to download latestHTML.zip and expand it into its directory
https://readthedocs.org/projects/dashboard-tactics-pi/downloads/

This is because it is not written in antora so I cannot add Dashboard_Tactics to the build.yml file to include asciidoc/antora processing of his docs.

Alec wrote:
You should (of course) push your changes on ocpn-manuals to github to make them visible. Probably not much idea to merge things yet (here, I'm upstream)

When I tried to push to my remote I had a fatal error because it did not exist.
Then I tried to push to my remote with git push upstream main --force still had a fatal error.
Then I forked your repository and tried git pull upstream main and I got conflicts because I did not use git rebase upstream main.
So then I used git merge --abort.
Now what? Should I switch git remote s to be origin --> rgleaon upstream --> leamas?
and git push origin main --force?

Well, if the remote is missing you need to add it. Its just 'git remote add <url>` For an upstream remote (a remote you don't have write access to) you use the https url. For a remote you can modify, you use the ssh address. Both are available in the github UI

http://mikerossiter.co.uk/OpenCPN/
Shows a dummy website built from the modules. There are holes and errors.

Now I can see the workflow better. @Alec Leamas is correct about using dev repos as the source, where they exist. Spending time with that today, using a couple of my plugins, where the modules will be added.

My work on modules has not been wasted because they can form the basis for PR from forks to dev repos. Where the repo cannot be contacted by PR my modules in the forks can substitute.

To keep my main github repos clear of this work intend using 'oplaydo' to host the forks. From these I can make the PR.
https://github.com/oplaydo

For editing VS Code has been brilliant. Need the asciidoc.js extension.
https://code.visualstudio.com

Firefox with the asciidoc extension has worked well. If anyone needs helping with viewing images please ask.

Back to the coalface.

Being a dumb Swede I need to Google "at the coalface"... Ah , right (and always interesting to learn!)

Skimmed through the manuals. This is indeed interesting, was lost for some time. And, it's huge. A lot of work, for sure...

"correct about using dev repos as the source"... well, if two such brilliant minds comes to the same conclusion it cannot possible be wrong, can it? (knock, knock...)

I guess it's to early for detailed feedback at this point. Perhaps we can come to a point where we have different people working with each plugin?! But before that, the overall organization needs to be in place, I know.

Editing: I have enabled the "Edit this page" link. It brings user to Github. Here she can click the pencil, change the asciidoc and preview the result. And when she saves, a PR is automagically created. It's sort of nice, but not a solution for our own needs internally.

You might be interested in the new UI bundle. It's basically just the default, scaled down, added a logo + some meaningful resource links. Available at https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-manuals/blob/main/ui-bundle.zip
Just removed a couple of modules from the main manual repo and replaced them by modules in the plugin repo, Working so far.
If I understand this correctly, it will take some time before you return from the mines and is fully talkable, right?
How to stop github making a build when a module is added to a plugin repo????
Disable the action temporary
Every time I disable actions it goes back to 'allow all actions'. Is there another switch somewhere?
No idea, never really tried that much
Off to do some house painting. BBL

It does exist, but that makes sense, I have to switch them. Upstream does not have write access.

Rick@Dart MINGW64 ~/documents/github/ocpn-manuals (main)
$ git remote -v
origin https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-manuals.git (fetch)
origin https://github.com/leamas/ocpn-manuals.git (push)
upstream https://github.com/rgleason/ocpn-manuals.git (fetch)
upstream https://github.com/rgleason/ocpn-manuals.git (push)

Easy enough to do

@Rick Gleason Those remotes seem a bit odd. When it comes to ocpn-manuals, I am upstream and you are downstream. This means that if we want to avoid all sorts of confusion origin should refer to rgleason/ocpn-manuals and upstream to leamas/ocpn-manuals
Great that you merged my PR. More under way.
The most pressing issue now is creating some sort of testplugin documentation. Is this something you can and want to do?

I will make a new PR and switch the remote. Ok.
Yes I plan on doing the documentation. Jon has redone his setup quite dramatically so I am integrating wetchdog with it to learn more and am writing the process up as I go, I hit a stumble last night and Jon is lokking at it.. I can put in the previous process tonight as a starter, it will change.

@Rick Gleason : Another PR is waiting! Found two watchdog folders of images in the site export. Had missed one of these. Some editing of the manual is still needed. I have included a 'build.yml' file. This works for a local build of the manual. The site: url is not important for this. Manual HTML files are in the docs folder.
(deleted)

Discussions on OpenCPN manuals
